body_txt: Musicals as an art form have a unique problem. For most musical fans, it merely falls under Willing Suspension of Disbelief . For those who dislike musicals and those musical fans with analytical minds, they have just one question on their minds: What's with all the singing? As we all know , in our world, music generally needs to be composed and practiced in advance. Rhyming poetry takes time to piece together. People don't just burst into song and dance in the middle of the street to express their feelings. Of course, a minority of gifted improvisers might be able to piece together some rhymes (freestyle rappers do it all the time) and carry a tune instantly, but how do you coordinate multiple singers and classically trained instrumentalists in such an improvisation? You simply cannot produce this kind of music spontaneously. So how do you make sense of a work of fiction where they do ?  The writers of musicals don't necessarily adopt exactly one of these interpretations for a work; rather, musicals often love to mix in more than one, especially with Diegetic or All In Their Heads songs sprinkled into a broader Alternate Universe or Adaptation Hypothesis work. After all, any reason to include a song is a good reason: we have musicals because we enjoy hearing stories told with music, regardless of why or how that music is there. Just go with it. Because this trope is a lump, all musicals are examples. When adding one, it is therefore necessary to detail which musical world hypothesis seems to apply to it overall and any notable song exceptions. See also Stylized for the Viewer .
